title: How Fast Do Investors Learn? Asset Management Investors and Bayesian Learning
abstract: We study the speed with which investors learn about managers’ skills by examining how quickly investor and managers’ beliefs converge. After showing our measure proxies for the change in the dispersion of beliefs, we find that hedge fund investors learn as fast as suggested by Bayes’ rule. However, we find mutual fund investors learn more slowly than suggested by Bayes’ rule. Mutual fund investors’ slow learning is not due to the use of different performance measures, institutional frictions, or lack of sophistication, but could be due to a low payoff from learning. Our results indicate learning speed depends on financial participants’ incentives.

author.Christopher Schwarz*: The Paul Merage School of Business, University of California at Irvine, USA

One difficulty all financial market participants face when making decisions is uncertainty. How investors resolve uncertainty through learning has important implications as it is an essential building block for several theoretical models in a variety of settings. For example,Pastor and Veronesi (2003,2006)find that learning can explain why higher growth rate uncertainty leads to higher stock prices.Timmerman (1983),Brennan and Xia (2001), andXia (2001)show that learning by investors can explain why stocks are more volatile than their underlying dividends. In the asset management literature,Berk and Green (2004)assume that mutual fund investors learn about manager skill from past fund performance, which explains why fund flows chase past returns while mutual fund returns are not persistent. These models generally assume that investors learn following Bayes’ rule. However, little work examines the speed at which financial market participants learn.

Thus, in this paper, we empirically study how quickly learning occurs in the asset management industry. Specifically, we examine how rapidly changes in the dispersion of investors’ beliefs about fund managers’ skill decline over time. Investors could have different priors about the skill of a manager; however, as they learn through common information, the dispersion of their beliefs should decline. Importantly, Bayesian learning would predict that the marginal impact a new piece of information on investors’ beliefs decreases as investors learn so the change in the dispersion of beliefs per period should decrease overtime. Thus, examining how quickly changes in the belief dispersion decline allows us to draw inferences about how fast investors learn about uncertain financial parameters.

Our novel measure of dispersion, which we call “IDAS” (investor dispersion about skill), is based on fund investor flows. Since money flows likely reflect changes in investors’ beliefs about a fund, we proxy for the change in dispersion of beliefs through the minimum of inflows and outflows.1The intuition is as follows. If investors all update their beliefs about the manager’s skill in the positive direction (i.e., is a good manager), we should observe high levels of inflows, but almost no outflows. If investors all update their beliefs about the manager’s skill in the negative direction (i.e., is a bad manager), we should observe high levels of outflows, but almost no inflows. In these two scenarios, all investors revise their belief in the same direction, resulting in little change in the dispersion of their beliefs. Correspondingly, the minimum of inflows and outflows will be near zero. If different investors update their beliefs in different directions, we should observe high levels of both inflows and outflows, and, therefore, the minimum of inflows and outflows will be nonzero. Thus, the larger the minimum of these two values, the larger the change in the dipersion of beliefs.

To validate our measure, we develop a theoretical framework in which investors hold dispersed prior beliefs about the skill of a fund manager and update their beliefs following Bayes’ rule when observing a common signal. In simulations based on this framework, we demonstrate that IDAS closely tracks the change in the dispersion of the beliefs, as they decline at approximately the same pace and have similar convex slopes. We also show that the standard error of fund alpha is a good empirical benchmark to compare IDAS against when examining whether investors’ learning speed is consistent with Bayesian learning.

Moving to our empirical analyses, we compare the actual learning speed implied by our IDAS measure against the expected learning speed implied by Bayes’ rule through the standard error of alpha. Interestingly, we find that mutual fund investors learn much more slowly as compared to Bayes’ rule. In fact, there is almost no decline in the change in the dispersion of beliefs for mutual fund investors even after observing over a decade’s worth of performance data. In contrast, we find that hedge fund investors learn at approximately the speed implied by Bayes’ rule, even though learning in this environment should be more difficult given the lower disclosure requirements for hedge funds. These conflicting results suggest that learning speed may depend on the particular investors involved in the financial decisions, as well as the conditions of the financial market they are involved in.

For example, investors’ learning speed may depend on their sophistication, access to informaiton, and incentive to learn, among other factors. To understand how these different factors affect learning, we examine the potential reasons mutual fund investors learn at a much slower pace than what Bayes’ rule suggests. The first potential explanation is a different interpretation of common information (e.g.,Barber, Huang, and Odean 2016). Although mutual fund investors all observe past fund returns, they may not evaluate a fund using the same risk-adjusted performance measure. For example, if a mutual fund manager appears skilled based on her excess style returns, but not her four-factor alpha, this could lead to persistent dispersion of beliefs.2However, we find that investors of funds with similar performance profiles across various performance measures have the same learning speed as those invested in funds whose performance measures disagree.

Another potential explanation is that front and rear loads impact learning speed. Loads impose hurdles to invest and divest in a fund, which could lead to less interest in learning about the fund. Additionally, loads sometimes represent payments to brokers to recommend funds to investors. Brokers also can be compensated through 12B1 fees. However, we find no difference in the speed of learning for funds with 12B1 broker fees and loads and those funds without 12B1 broker fees or loads. A third explanation is that, on average, mutual fund investors could learn slowly because many mutual fund investors are unsophisticated retail investors. However, we find that mutual funds with larger average account sizes (i.e., more institutional investor dominated funds) provide a similar empirical estimate of learning speed compared to other mutual funds.

Another possible way we can detect whether mutual fund investors learn about manager skill is by looking at manager changes. At the point of a manager change, the past performance information is not as useful in predicting the new manager’s skill. Thus, when we observe a change in manager, we should find a large increase in the dispersion of beliefs (hence a jump in the change in dispersion) since investors are learning about the skills of a new manager. However, we do not find any significant impact of manager changes on IDAS measures. This suggests that mutual fund investors are not attentive to manager changes, another piece of evidence that they do not learn about manager skill.3

Finally, we examine whether this inattention by mutual fund investors may be rational. Although lack of attention suggests a limitation on investors’ cognitive ability, investors can still be bounded rational in the sense that they allocate attention to learning about the most value relevant information (Sims 2003;Kacperczyk, van Nieuwerburgh, Veldkamp 2016). In other words, investors will only spend time learning if the effort from learning will be rewarding. We find that, if hedge fund investors invest in the best performing funds based on all historical excess returns compared to the funds with the lowest, they will outperform by 3%per year, which is statistically significant. Thus, it is beneficial for hedge fund investors to expend effort to learn. However, we find that mutual fund investors enjoy no significant performance advantage by investing in the best performing funds rather than the worst performing. Thus, mutual fund investors not learning could be rational since learning is not valuable. This could explain why mutual fund investors have poor monitoring; specifically, the monitoring is not useful and therefore investors do not put forth effort.

We have performed a number of additional robustness tests. For example, we may detect slow learning by mutual fund investors due to consistent contributions to tax-deferred retirement accounts. However, when we split our sample in half by the autocorrelation of inflows, which should be a symptom of these types of contributions, we find similar results. Also, to determine whether our results are driven by any particular set of funds, we separately examine the subsamples of live and dead funds, funds within each style, and funds incepted after the start of our sample period. We find results consistent with our main sample. We also use EVA (Berk and van Binsbergen 2015) as our measure of skill to account for the effect of fund size on performance. Again, we find slow learning. Finally, we also examine how the possibility of time-varying skills may affect the learning speed by using a time-varying skill measure (Kacperczyk, van Nieuwerburgh and Veldkamp 2014) to measure learning speed and by considering funds’ potential changes in strategy. In both cases, we do not find evidence our results are due to changes in manager skill over time. Our findings are also similar after controlling for any potential incubation bias (e.g.,Evans 2010). With our hedge fund sample, our findings are economically and stastistically consistent after controlling for any potential backfill bias (e.g.,Ackermann, McEnally, and Ravenscraft 1999), lockup periods, and assuring our results are not due to our annual observation frequency.

In summary, our results demonstrate that investors can learn as implied by Bayes’ rule, which is assumed by most financial theories based on learning. However, we find that learning likely depends on whether learning provides value to market participants. In the hedge fund industry, where monitoring is valuable, we find that investors learn via Bayes’ rule. However, mutual fund investors learn much more slowly. In fact, after more than a decade little learning has taken place. We find this slow learning is likely due to inattention, which could be rational since learning about the skill of a mutual fund manager appears not valuable for investors. Thus, assuming that investors learn about parameter uncertainty as predicted by Bayes’ rule may not always be appropriate, especially if it is not clear that learning is valuable.

Our paper is related to two other papers in the mutual fund space. BothHuang, Wei, and Yan (2022)andChoi, Kahraman, and Mukherjee (2016)examine mutual fund investors and learning. Generally, our results are consistent as both papers find that mutual fund investors learn to certain degree as we do.4However, our paper differs from the existing ones in several aspects. First, we focus on the convergence of the dispersion of beliefs as evidence of learning rather than net-flow-performance sensitivity. While documenting investors allocating more capital to past winners is consistent with learning, return-chasing behavior also can be attributed to behavioral biases (Frazzini and Lamont 2008;Bailey, Kumar, and Ng 2011). Our measure offers an alternative test on whether investors learn, which cannot be captured by net flow.5Second, in addition to studying whether mutual fund investors learn, we also examine the speed at which they learn. We find that learning eventually occurs, but mutual fund investors learn very slowly as compared to Bayes’ rule. Finally, our paper also evaluates learning of hedge funds investors and compares that to mutual fund investors.
We use a variety of data sources in this study. For mutual funds, we begin with the CRSP Survivorship Bias Free Mutual Fund database. As with prior mutual fund studies, we retain portfolios whose funds have an equity related style code in CRSP, as well as at least 50%of their portfolios invested in equities, valid data for the prior year’s December return and net asset fields, turnover, and expenses.6We then match these funds to their NSAR filings on the SEC’s EDGAR website. Since funds are required to file NSAR documents twice per year, we match our fund list against NSARs with an end date in the first half of the year and with an end date in the second half of the year. NSAR filings are not made based on the calendar year. Rather NSAR disclosures are made as of the halfway point and end of the fiscal year.

Because prior research has found a large number of errors on the NSAR documents (e.g.,Christoffersen, Evans, and Musto 2013;Cashman et al. 2014), we remove a match if the difference between its NSAR and CRSP net assets is larger than 10%⁠. In a majority of cases, we believe the match is correct, but exclude these data out in an abundance of caution. Across time, the quality of our matching does vary somewhat, although our lowest post-filter match rate is still approximately 87%⁠, while our highest postfilter match rate is 94%⁠. Thus, we are able to match a supermajority of funds, and our sample is unlikely to be subject to a severe selection bias.

Most of the data used in this study come from the CRSP mutual fund database. However, the advantage of the NSAR filings is that they contain information on the actual dollar inflows and outflows (question #28) into the fund. This allows us to directly observe the outside demand for the fund, as well as the inside investors that left the fund. These data are reported on a monthly basis. Our sample period for the mutual fund analysis is from 1996 to 2014.

For our hedge fund data, we use three sources of data. The first is SEC Form D. Form Ds include several descriptive items, including the name of the issuer, the fund entity type, the types of exemptions claimed, the date of first sale, the minimum investment amount, the total number of investors, and the cumulative amount sold (to all investors, not just U.S. investors). The investor and sales number represent the total history instead of current numbers, such as assets under management (AUM) or current number of investors. Thus, using consecutive filings, we can compute the amount of inflows as the difference between total historical sales from timetandt−1⁠.7

We collected the electronic forms from the SEC’s website over the April 2009 to June 2016 period. Scanned forms from January to March 2009 were added to our registry to complete the 2009 calendar year. To identify hedge funds, we focus on Form Ds indicating that the offering is within the “pooled investment fund” industry group, with subcategory defined as either “hedge fund” or “other investment fund.” We then match Form D hedge funds to two commercial hedge fund databases: TASS and HFR.8We use the February 2016 version of the TASS database, which has 20,069 funds, and the February 2016 version of the HFR database, which has 23,396 funds. Both databases contain live and defunct funds since 1994, which eliminates survivorship bias after that date. Funds common to both databases are identified using fund names, with characteristic data defaulting to HFR. The final database consists of 34,078 individual funds. However, not all of these funds can be matched to our Form D sample since our Form D filings start largely in 2009. Since 2009, only 24,242 funds have performance information in our combined database. Additionally, as is usual, we retain only funds denominated in U.S. dollars. This yields a final sample of 13,336 funds that we attempt to match to our Form D sample.

We match funds by fund names. Most of these funds are not in our Form D sample for two reasons. First, most are unavailable since our Form D sample starts in 2009 rather than 1994 like our commercial databases. The second reason is that Form D contains all hedge funds that have at least one external U.S. investor. We match a total of 20,225 Form D filings (22%⁠) to HFR and TASS, related to 4,349 different funds in the Form D sample (15%⁠). This coverage is rather low in terms of percentages, even though this is still a large sample of funds.9Since we can estimate net flows from commercial databases as inSirri and Tufano (1998)and have inflow information from the Form D filings, we can also compute estimated outflows for our hedge fund sample (inflows minus net flows). Unlike mutual fund flows, which are available on an intrayear basis, our estimates of hedge fund flows are annual.

In summary, we have inflows and outflows for both mutual funds and hedge funds, although we only have annual flows for hedge funds over a shorter time period. In the next section, we describe how we use these data to measure the learning speed.
article.2. Investor Dispersion Measure and Simul:
2.1 Definition of IDAS measure

To determine whether investors are learning at the speed of Bayes’ rule, we need to measure the dispersion of beliefs, as well as a benchmark for how fast investors would learn whether they are applying Bayes’ rule. Unfortunately, neither of these measures is observable, and, thus, we need to develop reasonable proxies for both. For measuring the benchmark for the expected speed of learning under Bayes’ rule, by definition investors’ ability to learn would strongly depend on the clarity of the signals observed over time. A strong (weak) signal with little (much) noise would lead to a rapid (slow) updating of beliefs by investors and therefore quicker (slower) convergence of beliefs. In terms of observable signals, a natural proxy would be alpha. A higher standard error of alpha represents noisier signals, which would result in slower learning via Bayes’ rule.

To study the learning speed, we focus on the change in the dispersion of beliefs. At the start of a fund, the dispersion of beliefs is likely to be large since investors have limited information about the skill of the manager. As the first pieces of performance arrive, it is likely that investors make large updates to their beliefs and thus the change in the dispersion of beliefs is large. However, as they receive common new information and update their beliefs, their opinions about the skill of a manager should converge and the changes in their dispersion should decline. Thus, examining how quickly the changes in the dispersion decline allows us to draw inferences about how fast investors learn about uncertain financial parameters.

Investors’ beliefs about the skills of a fund manager would be naturally revealed through their investments in the fund. Thus, changes in investors’ investments (i.e., flows) reflect changes in their beliefs. To proxy for the change in the dispersion of beliefs, we calculate the smaller of the inflows and outflows as a fraction of funds’ assets: I D A S = M I N ( I n f l o w s , O u t f l o w s ) N e t A s s e t s .

If most investors update their beliefs in the same direction, the dispersion of the belief updating is small. In this case, we would likely only observe a small fraction of investors going against herd. Since IDAS captures the belief updating of the minority investors going against the herd, a low level of IDAS implies a low dispersion in belief updating, which implies a small change in the dispersion of beliefs. In contrast, if different investors update their beliefs in different directions (i.e., the dispersion in belief updating is high), then we would likely observe both inflows and outflows, and the minimum of the two would be high, which implies a large change in the dipersion of beliefs.10,11

In the next section, we create a theoretical framework and perform simulations to validate our proxies for the convergence of beliefs, as well as the expected learning speed under Bayes’ rule.

2.2 Theoritical framework and simulation of IDAS

Our theoretical framework is similar to that inHuang, Wei, and Yan (2022), except we assume sophisticated investors hold dispersed prior beliefs about the skills of a fund manager. We do not include naïve investors who blindly chase past performance or allocate money across funds based on factors that are unrelated to fund performance. These naïve investors are likely to exist in the market, but since their flows are likely to be unrelated to age of the fund, including them is not likely to change the shape of the curve for changes in the dispersion of beliefs with respect to time. We simply assume these investors have $100,000 invested in the fund.12

There are two assets, a risk-free asset and an actively managed mutual fund. The return on the risk-free asset is set to zero without loss of generality. The excess return of the mutual fund at timetisrt=α+εt⁠, whereαis the unobservable skill of the fund manager and is taken to be constant over time. The noiseεtis i.i.d over time with a normal distribution,N(0,σε2)⁠.

On date 0, investors hold heterogenous prior beliefs about the skill of the manager. Prior literature has emphasized the role of prior information in analyzing the performance of mutual funds and making investment decisions (Pastor and Stambaugh 2002a,b). Different priors could arise because of investors’ different cultural backgrounds, past experiences, and financial literacy. For example, the literature finds that other funds’ performance within the same fund family contains valuable information about the performance of a focal fund (Brown and Wu 2016). If investors do not pay equal attention to cross-fund learning within families, then there could be different beliefs about new fund manager’s skills. Also,Bailey, Kumar, and Ng (2011)document that mutual fund investors’ behavioral biases vary with profession, income, age, and past investment experience.Del Guercio and Reuter (2014)find that the retail mutual fund market is formed from two broad clienteles that value funds differently: self-directed investors and investors acting with the help of brokers. These issues could all lead to heterogenous priors.

Note thatα0reflects the average prior beliefs of the investors at time 0, which could be either equal to or different from the true skill level of the fund manager,α⁠.

We compute our IDAS measure as we do using the actual data.

Based on the theoretical framework above, we conduct several simulation analyses. In the simulation, we first generate a random mutual fund using the framework ofBrown et al. (1992). The fund’s beta is randomly generated from a N(0.96, 0.34) distribution that matches the distribution of betas in our sample. Idiosyncratic risk is then related to beta as in that paper.

For our baseline result, each fund has 100 investors whose prior beliefsα0iare distributed N(0%⁠, 2.2%⁠). We assume managers have no skill since that is the general conclusion of most research (e.g.,Fama and French 2010), which means in our baseline case the actual and the prior belief of skill levels are the same. We choose 2.2%as the dispersion of the prior across investors because it matches the cross-sectional standard deviation of the alpha of our sample using all of fund returns. Thus, it seems reasonable to assume this would match the distribution of priors for a new fund. We also assume at the start of the new fund, each investor believes the skill of the fund manager has a standard deviation of 2.2%⁠.

We generate daily returns for our fund from Jan 1996 to December 2015 to match our sample. We use the S&P 500’s daily returns for our market return and then compute returns using the fund’s specific beta and idiosyncratic risk levels. We assume the standard deviation of the new return signal equals the standard deviation of monthly alphas, which is 5.2%⁠. However, since daily signals should be more noisy than monthly signals, we multiple this by the square root of 30.

Each day we then compute inflows and outflows. We sum these over the course of the month, just as we observe in our actual data, and compute IDAS. In addition, we compute the standard error of alpha for the fund at the end of the month, as well as the change in the standard deviation of investor beliefs as a percentage based on the prior month’s level. We run this simulation for 2,200 random funds and take the average across all simulations. We plot the graph comparing IDAS and change in the standard deviation of beliefs to examine how well our measure captures the declining speed of investors’ belief dispersion. SeeFigure 1for the result.

Overall, inFigure 1, we find that our measure of IDAS matches the decline in the change of the standard deviation of investor beliefs, validating IDAS as a valid proxy for the change in the dispersion of beliefs. Both curves have a similar rate of decline, which is what we would expect for a good proxy. Importantly, this is also the expected results from Bayes’ rule.

Unfortunately, in our empirical data, while IDAS will give a good proxy for the change in dispersion of beliefs, we cannot observe the expected speed of the decline of the change under Bayes’ rule in our data. As mentioned in the prior section, we believe the standard error of alpha would be an appropriate benchmark. As the standard error of alpha declines, investors’ dispersion in beliefs should decline in a similar fashion. To examine whether this is the case, inFigure 2, we plot the change in the dispersion of beliefs and the standard error of alpha to examine how the two measures relate to each other in our model with Bayesian learning.

InFigure 2, we find that the standard error of alpha and the change in dispersion closely track each other under the framework of Bayesian learning. Thus, these results validate our empirical tests using IDAS to measure investors’ learning speed, as well as our use of the standard error of alpha as the benchmark for the expected change in the dispersion of beliefs.13

InInternet Appendix III, we report a number of additional stimulations. First, in our baseline simulation, we assume that investors’ prior beliefs are unbiased about the true level of manager skill. However, as noted previously, the actual skill of the manager and the initial beliefs of investors about the manager’s skill could be mismatched. To address this possible incongruity, we conduct several additional simulation analyses, varying the average prior belief of investors,α0⁠, while keeping the actual skill of the managersαat zero. Second, the learning speed could also depend on the dispersion of initial priors. Thus, we vary the standard deviation of the initial beliefs. Third, in the simulations reported previously, the dispersion in beliefs stems from heterogeneous priors by investors. We report simulations where the dispersion also occurs because investors do not use the same model for evaluation (i.e., some investors use returns and some alpha). We also report simulations in which some investors use 1 year of data and others use 5 years of data. Finally, we run simulations in which investors have two funds to choose from. In all cases, the simulations validate our use of IDAS as a proxy for the change in the dispersion of investor beliefs, as well as the standard error of alpha as our benchmark for Bayes’ learning speed.
article.3. Empirical Investigation of IDAS and I:
In the prior section, we established that IDAS proxies for the change in the dispersion of beliefs. We also established that the standard error of alpha proxies for the learning speed we would expect if investors are learning as fast as Bayes’ rule implies. In this section, we examine our measure empirically and then examine investor learning speed.

3.1 Empirical IDAS properties

Before using our measure to examine learning speed empirically, we evaluate some of the empirical properties of IDAS. Unlike our simulations in which all fund flows were due to changes in the beliefs about manager skill, here fund investors could in practice invest in or withdraw money from funds for other reasons. For example, some investors simply may be investing in a fund for exposure to a particular asset class. Brokers may be pushing a particular fund to their clients. In terms of outflows, investors may leave a fund because of liquidity needs or tax considerations. They may also not leave a fund because of the disposition effect, although some dispute its impact on the mutual fund industry (e.g.,Odean 1998;Chang, Solomon, and Westerfield 2015;Loos, Meyer, and Pagel 2020). Therefore, only a part of IDAS in our sample will be related to changes in the belief about manager skill. Thus, in this section, we investiage some empirical properities of IDAS.

One situation in which we should find higher levels of IDAS is when there is higher levels of dispersion of beliefs about manager skill. For example, we should see that IDAS is related to extreme returns in the subsequent period. In other words, funds with high levels of dispersion should be those funds that ex ante are expected to have high return uncertainty and thus high likelihood of extreme performance. To examine this relation between IDAS and returns, we rank funds by return within their style in quartertand split the funds into deciles. We then calculate the average IDAS value, as well as the average net flow in the prior quarter.Table 2, panel A, reports the average values for the 10 deciles.

We find that IDAS is highest for the lowest and highest performance deciles, which is consistent with IDAS being correlated with high dispersion of beliefs about manager skill. The results are intuitive. In general, it pays more to learn about uncertain assets than safe assets. In our context, fund investors might benefit more from learning in the lower and upper range of performance as the rewards of higher performance or avoiding low performance might be stronger for these more extreme performance ranges. Since IDAS reflects investors’ degree of learning, funds with higher IDAS should exhibit more extremely returns.14Interestingly, net flows cannot predict future extreme performance by mutual funds as net flows are very similar for all 10 deciles. This again documents that our measure contains distinct information from net flows.15

Second, we compare IDAS for active managed mutual funds and passive index mutual funds. If IDAS is mainly driven by dispersion in beliefs about manager skill, we could see that index funds have lower levels of IDAS as compared to active funds. However, it is arguable that index funds are more affected by asset allocation decisions due to expectations on future market returns and investor liquidity needs than active funds. Thus, if the dispersion capture by IDAS is mainly due to portfolio rebalancing or liquidity needs, we could observe a higher dispersion for index funds than active fund. To test which effect dominates, we run a test on passive and active funds where we control for potential size and age differences by sorting funds into deciles based on size and age and compare active and passive funds within the same deciles. Panel B ofTable 2reports these results.

We find that index funds have lower levels of IDAS than active funds. When examining funds by size, we find that index funds consistently have lower IDAS.These differences are smaller for the very largest funds; however, this is to be expected since large active funds likely generate more public information about manager skill, either through living for a long time or through monitoring by current investors, which should lead to lower the dispersion of beliefs. We find that IDAS is very consistent across fund size. Thus, our measure is not driven by either small or large funds. We find similar results when examining funds by age deciles. Index Funds’ IDAS is consistently lower than that of active funds.

3.2 Examining investor learning speed

Now that we have examined some of the properties of our IDAS measure, we explore how quickly investors learn under our measure compared with Bayes’ rule. For our mutual fund sample, we use four measures to evaluate skill. The first is theCarhart (1997)four factor alpha model, which is commonly used in the mutual fund literature. The second is the one factor model that only includes the market excess return. We also include two return-based measures, which may be used by the retail investors typical of mutual funds. The first simply uses the raw returns of the fund, while the second uses style-adjusted returns.16As Bayesian theories assume that investors consider all past information, we use all of the returns prior to the current month to estimate the fund’s alpha. As discussed in the simulation section, we calculate the standard error of these four performance measures to proxy for how the dispersion in beliefs should behave if investors conduct Bayesian learning.

To begin our analysis, we split funds into age bins. The first bin consists of funds from 0 to 3 years old. We then create eight more groups with each group including the subsequent 3 years of fund ages. The last group, which does not contain many observations, contains funds that are older than 27 years. We then compute the average IDAS, as well as the average standard error of all of our four performance measures for each age group. However, rather than simply compute averages across age, we double sort funds into deciles based on size and our age groups to control for the size effect since size and age are correlated. We then compute averages for the age deciles across the size deciles, and finally across time.

Table 3reports these averages. Panel A reports the results of our alpha based measures, whereas panel B reports the results of our return-based measures. In addition to reporting the averages of each group, we also report the cumulative percentage decline in the measure as compared to the youngest age group.17

To examine the difference in decline rates cross-sectionally, we examine the difference between the decline in IDAS and the decline in the standard errors of our performance measures. We first compute the average IDAS and standard error for each fund each year of its life. We then compute the cumulative decline from the first year of data for the fund in our sample to the current year for both our measures as a percentage of the original values. We then find the difference between these declines and use this value as the dependent variable in a regression. We make two adjustments to this measure. Since we may not observe the first year of data for a fund, we adjust our differences by the average amount of decline in the IDAS, standard error, or both variables in our sample observed up until the fund is that age.21The second adjustment we make is that we assume a 100%decline in change in the dispersion of beliefs for mutual funds occurs when IDAS declines by 58%of the original value. This matches the total decline we observe for mutual funds in the data.22

The independent variables of interest are fund age in panel A and then two dummy variables—Young fundandMiddle-aged fund—in panel B. For mutual funds (hedge funds), a young fund is younger than 9 (6) years old and a middle-aged fund is between 9 (6) and 20 (15) years old. We also include a number of other fund characteristics as controls, such as the fund’s net assets, expense ratio, and turnover. Additionally, we include the average IDAS for funds in the same style and funds in the same management company. We include style and time fixed effects and cluster standard errors by fund.Table A1in theInternet Appendixreports the results.

The regressions results are similar to the univariate results. We find that the difference between IDAS and standard error decline for mutual funds is largest for young funds and is still statistically larger for middle-aged funds than old funds. Thus, this corroborates the slow learning we observe with the prior tables for mutual funds. However, we do not see the same relation for hedge funds. For hedge funds, we do not observe any significant difference between the decline rates, a finding that is again consistent with our prior results.

The difference between the hedge fund and mutual fund results are not driven by a few factors that are specific to hedge funds. First, unlike the mutual fund data, our hedge fund flows are annual. To ensure our results are not driven by the difference in observation frequency, we rerun the mutual fund results using annual flows. The conclusions using monthly or annual flows are similar, suggesting our hedge fund results are not driven by the use of annual flows. Second, some hedge funds have lockup periods and gates that could affect outflows from funds. In untabulated results, we find similar results for funds with and without lockup periods. Additionally, as we will document later, there is no difference in learning speed between hedge funds strategies that rarely use gates as compared to those that used gates frequently during the financial crisis (Aiken, Clifford, and Ellis 2015).

Third, hedge funds often have high watermarks. Because each investor could potentially be at a different high watermark, their incentive fees and therefore net returns could be different. However, this would bias against us finding fast learning for hedge funds since the signals investors receive may be different. In practice these return differences due to high watermark are likely to be small. In our data, 88%of hedge funds either do not have a high watermark or hit a new all-time cumulative return high each year. Once a new high occurs, all current investors and new investors would be at the same high watermark. Additionally, if we applied a 20%incentive fee to S&P 500 returns over this period or a 25%incentive fee, the correlation between the two sets of returns is 0.9998. If we compare the S&P 500 returns with no incentive fee (i.e., below the high watermark) versus those with a 20%incentive fee, the return series correlation is 0.998.

Overall, we find that hedge fund investors learn as quickly as implied by Bayes’ rule, but mutual fund investors do not.23In the next section, we will investigate factors that may affect learning speed.
article.4. Factors that May Affect Investors’ Le:
In the prior section, we found that investors learn slowly compared to Bayes’ rule in the mutual fund industry whereas learning speed in the hedge fund industry is consistent with Bayes’ rule. The question becomes why in the mutual fund industry investors learn slowly relative to both hedge fund investors and Bayes’ rule. In this section, we investigate some potential reasons that learning could be impeded.

4.1 Use of performance measures

Regardless of which performance measure we examine, mutual fund investors exhibit non-Bayesian learning. One possible reason is that investors use different performance measures to determine skill. For example, some investors may use the one-factor alpha as their skill measure, while other investors may use Sharpe ratio as their measure of skill (e.g.,Barber, Huang, and Odean 2016). If these measures paint a different picture, then this could lead to a high level of persistent dispersion in investor beliefs. On the other hand, if all measures suggest the same skill level, then the use of different performance measures by investors cannot explain our results.

To examine this possibility, we examine the consistency of three performance measures: the fund’s one-factor, four-factor, and Sharpe ratio. Each period, we standardize these measures to a distribution with a mean of zero and standard deviation of one. We then group funds into one of six groups based on the value of the standardized performance measure: greater than 1.96, between 1 and 1.96, between 0 and 1, between−1 and 0, between−1.96 and−1, and finally less than−1.96. We then split funds into two groups. If a fund has all three performance measures within the same group we label ourInconsistentvariable as zero, and if the performance measures fall into different groups we assign one toInconsistent.

We first examine the relation between IDAS decline and standard error decline using our univariate analysis. We graph the IDAS and standard error of the four-factor alpha for both groups inFigure 5.

Even if all performance measures provide the same general performance profile for the fund, it appears that the learning speed is still much slower than Bayes’ rule would indicate. In fact, there is little difference between the curves of funds with consistent and inconsistent performance measures.

We formally test whether the learning speed between the two groups are statistically different inTable A2in theInternet Appendix. We use the same empirical framework asTable A1, panel B, in theInternet Appendix. However, in these models we add ourInconsistentvariable, as well as the interactions between it and our age variables. If slow learning is caused by inconsistent performance measurements, the noninteracted age variables should be insignificant, and the interaction of the age variables withInconsistentshould be positive and signficant.

Although the sign of the interaction terms are positive, they are not statistically significant. Also, the noninteracted age variables continue to be significantly positive. Thus, the non-Bayesian learning of mutual fund investors does not appear to arise due to the use of different performance measures by investors.24

4.2 Impediments to fund flows

Another factor that could affect learning speed, as well as our measure, is funds’ loads. Both rear loads and front loads are “in your face” expenses that may discourage investors from investing in the fund (e.g.,Barber, Odean, and Zheng 2005). If this occurs, then learning speed would be slow since many investors would not invest the resources to learn about them. Additionally, front and rear loads would also affect IDAS since it is based on the minimum of inflows and outflows. These loads likely negatively affect both inflows and outflows.

Front and rear loads are also indicative of investors using brokers to make investment decisions. Brokers may have an objective function that is not consistent with optimal learning. Thus, this is another reason funds with loads may have slower learning. Another channel through which brokers can be compensated is through mutual funds’ 12b1 fees. Thus, we split our sample into three categories: Funds with no loads, funds with loads, and funds with no loads and no 12B1 fees related to broker or sale representative compensation as indicated on their NSAR form.

We perform the same univariate analysis as done previously inFigure 5for each of our three groups.Figure 6graphs the results.

We find that funds with no loads still exhibit slow learning. In fact, we find no significant difference in learning speed between load and no-load funds.

4.3 Inattention

Another possible reason for the slow decline in IDAS is that investors do not pay attention to less salient information. For example,Sirri and Tufano (1998)document that investors have an overwhelming number of funds to choose from and that advertising helps reduce search costs for investors.Barber, Odean, and Zheng (2005)find that investors pay attention to fund loads more than expenses because they are more obvious to investors. Investors may also exert resources learning about a fund when they make their initial investment, but then not check on the fund and cease learning. For example,Bergstresser and Poterba (2002),Johnson (2010), andCashman et al. (2014)all find that mutual fund redemptions are invariant to performance.

One event that coud lead to a large change in the dispersion of beliefs about the manager’s skill is when the fund manager changes. Obviously, when the manager changes, the prior performance information of the fund is no longer as relevant as an estimate for the new manager’s skill.25However, manager information is not displayed as frequently as returns. For example, Yahoo! Finance provides performance information in the summary information, but not the name of the manager. Thus, if investors are paying attention to less salient information like the fund manager, we should see IDAS rise after a fund manager change.

To examine this issue, we use the CRSP manager date to detect fund manager changes. We calculate the average IDAS for the 12 months prior to the manager change and then the 12 months after the manager change. We then compare the average difference between the IDAS before and after the change to those funds that did not change managers. We report both univariate results (panel A) and results from a regression (panel B) where we control for time, style, and other fund characteristics. That the change of a fund manager could be a positive (a poor manager is replaced by a better one) or a negative signal (a good manager leaves the fund) could cause a large inflow but small outflow in the former case, and a large outflow but small inflow in the latter. In these cases, we may not observe an increase in IDAS, but should still observe a change in net flow. Thus, to see whether investors pay attention to the manager change, we also perform our univariate analysis using net flows (panel C) and the absolute value of net flows (panel D).Table 5reports these results.

4.4 Investor type

Unlike hedge funds, mutual funds are dominated by retail investors, who are assumed to be unsophisticated. For example, retail investors are restricted from investing in hedge funds due to high income and net worth requirements. Thus, it may not be surprising these investors do not pay attention to information events like manager changes. However, institutional mutual fund investors could pay more attention to their funds, which would enable them to learn at the speed implied by Bayesian learning. If so, funds dominated by large, institutional type investors may show faster learning speeds than those funds with more retail investors.

We therefore examine mutual funds that likely have high institutional ownership separately from those funds likely dominated by retail investors. Using the number of accounts information available on NSAR filings, we calculate the average account size for each mutual fund. We then rank funds by their average account size. Note for the interpretation of the variable, we inversely rank funds such that the fund with the largest account size has a rank of zero. We then interact this variable with our age related variables, andTable 6reports the results.

4.5 Strategy uncertainty

One reason hedge fund investors appear to learn faster than that of mutual funds may be that the initial uncertainty about hedge fund managers is much higher than the initial uncertainty about mutual fund managers, due to the large heterogeneity of investment strategies they pursue. However, because of the complication of the hedge fund strategies, investors’ signal about manager skills also may be noisier, and, therefore, the Bayesian updating may be slower. Thus, ultimately it becomes an empirical question about whether investors learn faster or more slowly for funds with more complex strategy.

To investigate this issue, we look at the learning speed of various hedge funds strategies.27Figure A8in theInternet Appendixplots the IDAS and standard error curves for Relative Value, Macro, Event Driven, and Equity Hedge. These figures reveal some interesting results. First, we see in all cases, the two curves again match closely like they do for our overall sample. Second, the higher the standard deviation of alpha, for example with Relative Value, the higher our measure of IDAS, which is consistent with more uncertain strategy being associated with higher initial dispersion of beliefs. Third, and most importantly, in terms of how learning speed relates to the ex ante uncertainty about the hedge fund managers, we do not find a monatonic relation between initial strategy uncertainty and learning speed. On the one hand, we find investors for relative value funds (higher uncertainty strategy funds) learn faster than those for event driven funds (lower uncertainty strategy funds). On the other hand, investors for relative value funds and macro funds learn at a similar speed, despite that relative value funds have a higher strategy uncertainty.

4.6 Potential of time-varying skills

Another potential result for slow learning by mutual fund investors is that manger skill varies over time. If we think about the source of time-varying manager skill, three potential drivers emerge. The first is the manager is gaining experience as she manages the fund. However, any potential mutual fund manager likely has extensive management experience prior to getting a job as a mutual fund manager. Additionally, experience would likely cause convex changes in manager skill changes. In other words, the first year of experience would change skill much more than the fifth year. However, even by year 10 we do not find learning by mutual fund investors. Thus, this type of skill change seems not to be the cause our results.

The second potential driver of skill changes is manager switching their strategy. If a manager’s skill is strategy specific, then as fund changes strategy the skills might change as well. When a manager changes strategy, they likely change how they manage the portfolio. Thus, to examine whether changing strategy has any effect on investors’ learning speed, we use the NSAR data to identify funds that are switching strategies. One of the questions in NSAR form asks for what investment activities are allowed and whether they are used that period. We count the number of answers that change over the life of the fund and then index it by the number of years of the fund (i.e., average changes per year) and split funds into two groups based on whether they are below (panel A) or above the median (panel B).28Figure A9in theInternet Appendixreports the results. We find that investors do not learn much over the first 15 years no matter whether the funds change strategy or not. For low product change funds, IDAS is averaged at 2.2%for years 1–3 and only decreases to 2.0%by 16–18 years old; for high product change funds, the corresponding IDAS numbers are 2.9%vs. 2.6%for years 1–3 versus years 16–18, respectively.

Finally, the third potential driver of skill changes is that manager skill depends on market conditions. Perhaps in some markets one type of skill is more valuable and vice versa. This issue is very similar to the situation described inKacperczyk, van Nieuwerburgh and Veldkamp (2014), who find that skilled mutual fund managers focus on stock picking in booms and market timing in recession. Thus, to investigate the impact of this type of skill change, we calculate their measure each month and then use this measure’s standard error as our benchmark for Bayes’ learning. The idea is that if investors are aware that managers’ skill changes with the business cycle, then this skill measure should be a more informative signal for the investors to focus on. Thus the standard error of this skill measure becomes a more relevant benchmark for evaluating the speed of learning.Figure A10in theInternet Appendixvisualizes the results. Again, we conclude mutual fund investors learn slowly.

Overall, these results suggest that time-varying manager skills may not be the main driver of the slow learning for mutual fund investors.29

4.7 Value to learning

So far, the most likely reason we have identified for mutual fund investors’ slow learning is inattention. This does not seem rational as one would expect that mutual fund investors are motivated to learn about manager skill. To examine how strong the incentive to learn is, we examine the value to learning for both hedge fund and mutual fund investors. Each period, we rank funds by thet-value of their performance measure using all available returns. We then sort funds into deciles based on theset-values and find the difference between the highest and lowest deciles. While similar to performance persistence tests run previously in the literature, our tests use all prior returns rather than returns over a shorter-term period like one quarter or one year.Figure 7reports the results.

Figure 7 Value to learning This figure plots the returns from learning. Each quarter, we compute all funds historical excess return t -values. We then group funds into deciles based on these t -values. We then compute the following quarter returns of the top minus bottom decile. This figure displays the cumulative returns from this strategy for both hedge funds and mutual funds. Open in new tab Download slide

Our results suggest that mutual fund investors have little reason to learn.30Over the entire 15-year period, the cumulative difference between the best and worstt-value funds is approximately 12%⁠. This translates to a performance difference of 0.2%per quarter, which is insignificant. On the other hand, hedge funds have a cumulative performance difference of 45%over the same period, or 0.8%per quarter. This difference is significant at the 1%level. Thus, hedge fund investors have an incentive to learn.31

Overall, these results can explain the learning difference between hedge fund and mutual fund investors. Mutual fund investors do not pay attention to fund performance because there is no value to learning, while hedge fund investors are motivated to learn and therefore do so as efficiently as possible. While this suggests mutual funds are rationally inattentive, we cannot rule out the possibility that even if it was valuable to learn mutual fund investors will still be inattentive.32
article.5 Conclusion:
Many models in finance assume that investors learn over time. These models help to explain a number of empirical anomalies. For example, learning can explain why mutual fund performance does not persist, even if managers are skilled. Most of these models expect investors to learn according to Bayes’ rule. However, little evidence exists concerning whether investors learn that quickly.

In this paper, we use a new measure called IDAS to proxy for the change in the dispersion of investor beliefs to examine how quickly investors learn. After demonstrating using simulation analyses that our measure proxies for investors learning about manager skill through a decline in the change of the dispersion of beliefs, we provide evidence that hedge fund investors can learn as quickly as Bayes’ rule implies. However, we also document mutual fund investors learn much slower than Bayes’ rule. While the standard error of alpha, our proxy for the expected speed of learning from Bayes’ rule, declines substantially over the first 10 years of a fund’s life, the change in the dispersion of beliefs as measured by IDAS does not decline significantly over this same time period. Only after a mutual fund exists for more than 15 years do the declines in IDAS and the standard deviation of alpha become more aligned for mutual fund investors.

Since learning speed is heterogeneous, we then investigate why mutual fund investors learn relatively slowly as compared to Bayes’ rule. We show their leaning speed is not due to investors using different performance measures or due to investment frictions like front and rear loads. Rather, we find evidence consistent with slow learning due to inattention. While one may assume inattention is due to retail investors, we find that even mutual funds dominated by institutional investors learn slowly. However, we show that inattention by mutual fund investors may be rational. While hedge fund investors gain value from learning, learning about mutual fund manager skill is not valuable, which may dissuade mutual fund investors from exerting effort to learn.

Overall, our results suggest that learning speed is heterogeneous across different environments. Investors will not spend time learning if that learning does not have any payoff. More specifically to the mutual fund industry,Berk and Green (2004)suggest that mutual fund performance is not persistent due to learning about manager skill. We find investors exhibit persistent differences of opinions about a fund, which suggests they may not learn as fast as assumed by their model. However, it is unclear how incorporating dispersion in beliefs and slow learning would affect the equilibrium results of their model. This could be an interesting direction for future research.
